threats.title: batchTransferOutAndCallV5 Reuses msg.value for Each ETH Aggregation Call, Enabling Double-Spend or Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: In batchTransferOutAndCallV5, each _transferOutAndCallV5 call for ETH (fromAsset == address(0)) uses msg.value as the value sent to the aggregator. If a batch contains N ETH-based entries, the first call sends all msg.value; subsequent calls attempt to send msg.value again, either draining pre-existing router ETH or causing cascading fallback failures.
threats.investigation_rationale: msg.value is a transaction-level constant but is used per-iteration. The first aggregator receives all ETH; subsequent aggregators receive msg.value from the router's residual balance, which may include ETH from unrelated operations or cause reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transferOutAndCallV5 uses msg.value in target.call{value: msg.value} inside a loop; msg.value is constant per transaction

preconditions: Batch contains >=2 entries where fromAsset == address(0)
Vault constructs and sends the batch transaction

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Each iteration of the batch loop sends full msg.value to aggregator target via .call{value: msg.value}
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: THORChain validator nodes construct batch payloads
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any batch with multiple ETH aggregation entries

operational_questions: Do THORChain nodes ever batch multiple ETH-based aggregation calls?
What happens to excess ETH sent to the first aggregator?
Can router's pre-existing ETH be drained by subsequent calls?
validation_requirements: Construct batch with 2 ETH entries, verify first gets all ETH
Verify fallback cascades for second entry
Test if router ETH balance can be stolen

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe or trigger conditions where router holds residual ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If a vault batch includes multiple ETH aggregation calls, first call drains msg.value plus any pre-existing ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Subsequent calls in batch attempt to re-send msg.value from depleted balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Fallback to msg.sender.transfer may revert, blocking entire batch
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for DoS; requires vault batch construction for fund loss

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Batch outbound transactions with multiple ETH swaps fail entirely
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Outbound queue blocked, user funds delayed

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - triggered by normal batch construction
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time batch contains multiple ETH aggregation calls

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed batch transactions, unexpected ETH accumulation in aggregators
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Partial batch execution or full revert
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple ETH entries in single batch calldata

threats.title: _transferOutAndCallV5 ETH Fallback Sends to Aggregator Target Instead of Recipient
threats.hypothesis_description: When the aggregator's swapOutV5 call fails in the ETH path of _transferOutAndCallV5, the fallback sends ETH to aggregationPayload.target (the aggregator contract) instead of aggregationPayload.recipient (the intended user). This differs from V4 behavior which correctly sends to the user.
threats.investigation_rationale: The fallback line is `payable(aggregationPayload.target).send(msg.value)` but should be `payable(aggregationPayload.recipient).send(msg.value)`. If the aggregator lacks a rescue function or its owner is unresponsive, user ETH is permanently lost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payable(aggregationPayload.target).send(msg.value) sends to aggregator not recipient on swap failure

preconditions: Aggregator swapOutV5 call fails (slippage, pool drained, etc.)
fromAsset == address(0) (ETH path)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: V4 transferOutAndCall sends to `to` on failure; V5 sends to `target` — behavioral regression
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - triggered by normal swap failure conditions
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any failed aggregator swap in V5 ETH path

operational_questions: How often do aggregator swaps fail in production?
Do whitelisted aggregators have rescue functions?
What is the cumulative value at risk?
validation_requirements: Deploy failing aggregator, call transferOutAndCallV5, verify ETH goes to aggregator not recipient
Compare V4 vs V5 fallback behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Aggregator swap fails due to market conditions or intentional revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: ETH sent to aggregator contract instead of intended recipient
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If aggregator has no accessible rescue mechanism, funds are locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User receives no ETH and no tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - occurs naturally on swap failure

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User fund loss on every failed V5 ETH aggregation swap
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Depends on aggregator having rescueFunds function
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All transferOutAndCallV5 ETH paths where swap fails

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ETH accumulating in aggregator contracts unexpectedly
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: User complaints about missing ETH after swap failures
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Failed swapOutV5 calls followed by ETH at aggregator

threats.title: _transferOutAndCallV5 ERC20 Path Has No Fallback — Tokens Permanently Stranded in Aggregator on Swap Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: In the ERC20 path of _transferOutAndCallV5, tokens are transferred to the aggregator before calling swapOutV5. If swapOutV5 fails, the result (_dexAggSuccess) is intentionally ignored and the transaction succeeds. However, there is no fallback to return tokens to the recipient or vault. Tokens are permanently stranded in the aggregator.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code comment says 'if the aggregator fails, the transaction should not revert' but provides no alternative token recovery path. The vault's allowance is already decremented and tokens are at the aggregator with no return mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ERC20 transferred to aggregator, swapOutV5 called, _dexAggSuccess unchecked, no fallback path

preconditions: fromAsset != address(0) (ERC20 path)
Aggregator swapOutV5 fails after receiving tokens

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Token transfer to aggregator succeeds (required), but swap call failure is silently ignored

operational_questions: Can a malicious aggregator intentionally fail swaps to accumulate tokens?
Do all whitelisted aggregators have rescueFunds accessible by a trusted party?
What is the frequency of ERC20 aggregator swap failures?
validation_requirements: Call _transferOutAndCallV5 with an aggregator that reverts swapOutV5
Verify tokens stuck in aggregator, vault allowance decremented, no recovery

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Aggregator contract registered via fuzzy-match receives ERC20 tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tokens remain in aggregator; router considers transfer complete
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Aggregator owner calls rescueFunds to extract tokens (if malicious)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Malicious aggregator needs to pass THORNode whitelist fuzzy-matching

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User ERC20 tokens lost on every failed V5 aggregation swap
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires aggregator owner cooperation via rescueFunds
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ERC20 transferOutAndCallV5 where aggregator swap fails

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low for natural failures; medium for intentional exploitation
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic for natural occurrence; moderate for malicious aggregator

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ERC20 token balance accumulation at aggregator addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Successful router transactions where recipient received nothing
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated swapOutV5 failures from specific aggregators

threats.title: Permissionless swapOutV5 on Aggregator Enables Draining Stuck ERC20 Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: THORChain_Aggregator.swapOutV5 is public with no access control. In the ERC20 path, it approves the swapRouter for fromAmount and calls swapExactTokensForETH using a low-level .call (making the aggregator the msg.sender to swapRouter). Any caller can specify fromAsset and fromAmount matching tokens held by the aggregator, causing the swapRouter to pull those tokens from the aggregator and swap them to ETH sent to the attacker's recipient address.
threats.investigation_rationale: Tokens accumulate in the aggregator from failed _transferOutAndCallV5 ERC20 swaps (OPS-H003). An attacker can drain these tokens by calling swapOutV5 directly with the right parameters.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function approves swapRouter for fromAmount of fromAsset, then calls swapExactTokensForETH via low-level .call; no access control, no validation of token source

preconditions: Aggregator holds ERC20 tokens (from failed swaps, direct transfers, etc.)
Swap route exists on swapRouter for fromAsset -> WETH
Sufficient liquidity on swapRouter

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: swapOutV5 approves and swaps from aggregator's own balance without verifying tokens were received in the current call
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time aggregator holds ERC20 tokens

operational_questions: How much ERC20 value typically accumulates in aggregators from failed swaps?
Is there monitoring for direct swapOutV5 calls not from the router?
validation_requirements: Send ERC20 tokens to aggregator, call swapOutV5 from attacker address, verify tokens drained to attacker

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor aggregator for stuck ERC20 token balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Aggregator approves swapRouter for the amount and calls swap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: SwapRouter pulls tokens from aggregator, sends ETH to attacker

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All stuck ERC20 tokens in aggregator drained
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Irreversible — tokens swapped and sent to attacker
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any ERC20 tokens held by aggregator

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When aggregator holds tokens of value

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: swapOutV5 calls not originating from router contracts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden draining of aggregator ERC20 balances
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct calls to swapOutV5 with exact aggregator balance amounts

threats.title: Aggregator swapIn Uses Full Contract ETH Balance, Enabling Theft of Accumulated ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn and AvaxAggregator.swapIn, after the DEX swap, `_safeAmount = address(this).balance` is used to determine how much ETH/AVAX to deposit into the THORChain router. This includes any pre-existing ETH in the aggregator (from failed swapOuts, direct sends, or selfdestruct). An attacker can sweep all accumulated ETH by performing a minimal swap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The aggregator should only deposit the ETH received from the current swap, not its entire balance. Pre-existing balance from other users' failed operations can be stolen.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeAmount = address(this).balance; deposits entire balance, not just swap output

preconditions: Aggregator has pre-existing ETH/AVAX balance (from failed operations, donations, etc.)
Attacker has tokens to perform a valid swap through swapRouter
Swap route exists and produces non-zero ETH output

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: address(this).balance after swap includes all contract ETH, not just swap proceeds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time aggregator holds pre-existing ETH

operational_questions: How much ETH accumulates in aggregators from failed V5 swaps?
Can an attacker profitably sweep even small accumulated amounts?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to aggregator directly, then call swapIn with minimal tokens, verify full balance deposited

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor aggregator ETH balance for accumulated value
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Approve minimal tokens to aggregator and call swapIn
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Swap produces small ETH; address(this).balance includes pre-existing ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Full balance deposited to attacker-controlled vault+memo in THORChain router

potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Aggregator ETH balance from any source

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When aggregator balance exceeds swap cost

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: swapIn deposits exceeding expected swap output
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Aggregator balance drops to 0 after small swapIn calls
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Small token swaps that deposit disproportionately large ETH amounts

threats.title: batchTransferOutV5 ETH Accounting Mismatch: msg.value Not Validated Against Sum of ETH Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: In batchTransferOutV5, each _transferOutV5 for ETH uses transferOutPayload.amount (drawn from the contract's ETH balance which includes msg.value). There is no validation that msg.value equals the sum of all ETH amounts in the batch. Excess msg.value gets permanently locked in the router; deficit could drain any pre-existing router ETH balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The router's ETH balance after the batch should be unchanged (excluding msg.value). If msg.value > sum_of_eth_amounts, the difference is locked forever. If pre-existing ETH exists and msg.value < sum, it could be drained.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transferOutV5 uses transferOutPayload.amount for .send(), not msg.value; no validation msg.value == sum of ETH amounts

preconditions: msg.value != sum of ETH amounts across batch entries
OR router has pre-existing ETH balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No check that total ETH being sent equals msg.value provided by vault
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any batch with ETH entries where msg.value doesn't match

operational_questions: Does Bifrost correctly calculate msg.value for batches?
Does the router ever accumulate ETH from other sources?
Can excess ETH be recovered?
validation_requirements: Send batch with msg.value > sum of amounts, verify excess locked
Send batch with msg.value < sum, verify router ETH drained or revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: If router holds residual ETH from failed bounces
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Vault sends batch with msg.value < sum of ETH amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Router's pre-existing ETH supplements the shortfall
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pre-existing ETH effectively stolen by batch recipients
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires incorrect msg.value calculation by vault/Bifrost

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism for locked ETH in router

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero ETH balance in router contract

threats.title: AvaxRouter deposit() Is Public, Bypassing Expiry Protection in depositWithExpiry
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxRouter.deposit() is declared public (not private), unlike THORChain_Router where _deposit is private. Any caller can invoke deposit() directly, bypassing the block.timestamp < expiration check enforced by depositWithExpiry(). This allows stale transactions to execute without expiry protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: THORChain_Router V5 correctly makes _deposit private, only callable via depositWithExpiry. AvaxRouter inconsistently exposes deposit() as public, creating a divergence in security guarantees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: deposit() is public payable nonReentrant — no expiration check; THORChain_Router._deposit is private

preconditions: User or integration calls deposit() directly instead of depositWithExpiry()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public deposit() function accessible to anyone without expiry guard

operational_questions: Do any frontends or integrations call deposit() directly?
What is the impact of executing stale deposits?
validation_requirements: Call deposit() directly on AvaxRouter, verify no expiry check

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transaction remains in mempool during congestion
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction executes long after user intended, at unfavorable rates
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User receives worse swap rate than intended

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users may execute stale deposits at bad rates
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All direct deposit() calls on AvaxRouter

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to deposit() vs depositWithExpiry() ratio
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct deposit() calls from user wallets

threats.title: AvaxAggregator Has No Owner or Rescue Function — Stuck Funds Permanently Lost
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxAggregator has no owner, no rescueFunds function, and no mechanism to recover stuck tokens or AVAX. Unlike THORChain_Aggregator which has owner + rescueFunds, any assets stuck in AvaxAggregator (from failed swaps, direct sends, etc.) are permanently irrecoverable.
threats.investigation_rationale: THORChain_Aggregator has rescueFunds(). AvaxAggregator does not. Both can accumulate stuck assets from the same failure modes (swapIn using address(this).balance, direct ETH/AVAX sends). The asymmetry means AvaxAggregator has zero recovery capability.
threats.config_evidence: deploy.ts deploys AvaxAggregator with no post-deploy owner setup or rescue capability

proof_of_signal.pattern: No owner variable, no rescueFunds function, no withdrawal mechanism; has receive() payable accepting arbitrary AVAX

preconditions: Any AVAX or ARC20 tokens sent to or stuck in AvaxAggregator

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contract accepts ETH via receive() but has no extraction mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin exists — nobody can rescue funds
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — any stuck funds are lost forever

operational_questions: How much value has accumulated in AvaxAggregator historically?
Can the swapIn balance-sweep pattern (OPS-H005) be used to extract stuck AVAX?
validation_requirements: Send AVAX directly to AvaxAggregator, verify no recovery mechanism
Compare with THORChain_Aggregator which has rescueFunds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack — a permanent fund loss condition
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any failed swap or direct send results in permanently locked funds

potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All AvaxAggregator operations that could leave residual funds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero AVAX/token balance at AvaxAggregator address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Growing balance with no withdrawal transactions

threats.title: ETH_RUNE transferTo Uses tx.origin Enabling Unauthorized Token Theft Via Any Intermediate Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: ETH_RUNE.transferTo() uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender as the source of funds. Any contract that a user interacts with in ANY transaction can call transferTo() to drain the user's entire ETH.RUNE balance without approval, since tx.origin points to the original transaction signer regardless of the call chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a known dangerous pattern (SWC-115). Unlike approval-based attacks, the victim doesn't need to have approved anything — simply interacting with any contract that calls transferTo is sufficient to lose all RUNE tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transfer(tx.origin, recipient, amount) — tx.origin used instead of msg.sender

preconditions: Victim holds ETH_RUNE tokens
Victim interacts with any contract that calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo()
ETH_RUNE is not deprecated (per docs it's transitionary)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transferTo explicitly uses tx.origin with code comment acknowledging the risk
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any deployed contract can exploit this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any user transaction involving a malicious contract

operational_questions: Is ETH_RUNE still in active use?
How much value is held in ETH_RUNE?
Has this been exploited historically?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract that calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo in any function
Have victim call attacker contract, verify RUNE drained

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy a contract with attractive functionality that internally calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo(attacker, type(uint).max)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User interacts with the contract for any reason
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: ETH_RUNE.transferTo drains victim's entire balance to attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attack is invisible to the victim during the transaction
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires victim to interact with attacker contract

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of user's ETH.RUNE tokens

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — deploy contract and attract users

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: transferTo calls from contract addresses not known as upgraders
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large transferTo amounts to non-upgrade contracts

threats.title: Router _routerDeposit safeApprove Incompatible with USDT — Blocks Vault Migration for USDT
threats.hypothesis_description: _routerDeposit calls safeApprove(asset, router, amount) without first resetting to 0. USDT's approve() requires the current allowance to be 0 before setting a new non-zero value. If any stale allowance exists to the new router address, the approve call fails, blocking vault migration for USDT.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDT is explicitly in scope per project docs. The Router is expected to handle 'weird' ERC20 behaviors. A stale allowance from a previous failed migration or griefing attack would permanently block USDT migration to a new router.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeApprove(_asset, _router, _amount) without prior safeApprove(_asset, _router, 0); USDT requires approve(0) first

preconditions: Asset is USDT or similar token with approve race protection
Stale allowance exists from previous operation or griefing attack
Migration to new router is attempted via _routerDeposit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: safeApprove sets non-zero without clearing to 0 first
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During vault churn/router migration for USDT

operational_questions: Has USDT migration been tested in production?
Can stale allowances be created via failed migrations?
Does THORChain currently hold USDT in the router?
validation_requirements: Create stale USDT allowance on router, attempt _routerDeposit, verify revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Failed or interrupted router migration leaves stale USDT approval
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Subsequent migration attempt calls safeApprove with non-zero amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: USDT approve reverts due to non-zero existing allowance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: USDT funds locked in old router, cannot migrate to new router
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None for natural occurrence; low for griefing

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires manual allowance reset or alternative migration path
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: transferAllowance, returnVaultAssets for USDT to different router

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Non-zero USDT allowance from router to another router address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated migration failures for specific tokens

threats.title: THORChain_Aggregator swapIn Approves Original Amount Instead of Safe Amount for Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn, safeTransferFrom returns _safeAmount (actual received after fees), but safeApprove uses the original `amount` parameter, not `_safeAmount`. For fee-on-transfer tokens, this creates a stale over-approval to swapRouter that persists after the swap completes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The over-approval is `amount - _safeAmount` which remains on the swapRouter. While this doesn't directly cause fund loss, it means the swapRouter retains an allowance to spend aggregator tokens, creating a lingering attack surface.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeApprove(token, address(swapRouter), amount) uses original amount; swapExactTokensForETH uses _safeAmount; difference remains approved

preconditions: Token is a fee-on-transfer token
Fee-on-transfer reduces received amount below requested amount

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Approve uses amount, swap uses _safeAmount, leaving (amount - _safeAmount) approved to swapRouter
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After any swapIn with fee-on-transfer tokens

operational_questions: Which fee-on-transfer tokens are supported by THORChain?
Can the stale approval be exploited by the swapRouter?
validation_requirements: Execute swapIn with fee-on-transfer token, check remaining approval to swapRouter

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify fee-on-transfer tokens used with the aggregator
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Multiple swapIn calls accumulate stale approvals
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If aggregator later holds those tokens, swapRouter could pull them via existing approval
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Combined with OPS-H004, compounds the risk

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Residual approval could enable unauthorized token spending

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero token allowances from aggregator to swapRouter after completed swaps
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Approval amounts don't match swap amounts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Gradually increasing allowances for fee-on-transfer tokens
